Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptic nominal clause; appamādo as nominative subject, amatapadaṃ as nominative predicate metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Diligent heedfulness is the reliable way toward safety, wisdom, and lasting benefit.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Satiyā ca vīriyena ca kiccaṃ kātabbaṃ.
Usage Contexts: ethics; education; monastic training; leadership; personal discipline
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Well-known Dhammapada-style expression in the Theravāda textual tradition; it contrasts wakeful discipline with careless decline.
First Example Sentence: Ācariyo antevāsikaṃ ovadi: “appamādo amatapadaṃ, tasmā ajja yeva sippaṃ uggaṇhāhi.”
Second Example Sentence: Bhikkhu rattiyaṃ ca divā ca bhāvanāya yutto hutvā appamādo amatapadanti sarati.
Third Example Sentence: Gahapati putte āmantesi: “vohāre appamādo amatapadaṃ, pamādena dhanaṃ nassati.”

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptic nominal clause; pamādo as nominative subject, maccuno as genitive possessor, padaṃ as predicate noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Negligence leads toward ruin, danger, or moral death.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Asatiyā ca kosajjena ca anattho uppajjati.
Usage Contexts: warnings; moral instruction; risk; household advice; monastic discipline
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Traditional counter-formula to appamādo amatapadaṃ, associated with canonical moral instruction on heedfulness.
First Example Sentence: Navako vihāre pamādo maccuno padanti sutvā cīvaraṃ saṃvidahi.
Second Example Sentence: Rājapuriso senaṃ ovadi: “pamādo maccuno padaṃ; dvāraṃ rakkhatha.”
Third Example Sentence: Kassako meghaṃ disvā khippaṃ bījaṃ nikkhipi, pamādo maccuno padanti cintento.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Emphatic nominal clause; attā as subject, attano as reflexive genitive, nātho as predicate, hi as emphatic particle.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: One must take responsibility for one’s own conduct and welfare.
Usage Contexts: self-discipline; counseling; education; family; ethical reflection
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Famous canonical-style maxim emphasizing personal responsibility in the Pali ethical tradition.
First Example Sentence: Mātā puttaṃ āha: “attā hi attano nātho; tvaṃ yeva saccaṃ vadehi.”
Second Example Sentence: Bhikkhunī dukkhitaṃ upāsikaṃ samassāsesi: “attā hi attano nātho; kusalaṃ ārabha.”
Third Example Sentence: Seṭṭhi dāsaṃ na tajjesi, atha naṃ ovadi: “attā hi attano nātho, kammaṃ suṭṭhu karohi.”

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal predication with plural predicate relation; mano as conceptual head, pubbaṅgamā as adjective, dhammā as plural noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Mental intention shapes speech, action, and experience before outward results appear.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Cittaṃ paṭhamaṃ pavattati, tato vacīkammaṃ kāyakammañca hoti.
Usage Contexts: psychology; meditation; ethics; education; discourse analysis
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Opening-type Dhammapada expression widely recognized in Theravāda learning communities.
First Example Sentence: Thero antevāsike pucchi: “kiṃ tumhe jānātha, mano pubbaṅgamā dhammā?”
Second Example Sentence: Yuvā kujjhitvā tuṇhī ahosi, mano pubbaṅgamā dhammāti saranto.
Third Example Sentence: Vaidyo roginaṃ samassāsesi: “mano pubbaṅgamā dhammā; cittaṃ pasādehi.”

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative finite verbal clause; veraṃ as neuter subject/object concept, verena as instrumental means, na sammati as negated present verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Hostility cannot be resolved by more hostility.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Doso dosena na vūpasammati; mettāya so vūpasammati.
Usage Contexts: conflict resolution; politics; family disputes; diplomacy; sermons
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Prominent Dhammapada maxim on non-retaliation; central to Buddhist moral rhetoric.
First Example Sentence: Dve kulāni vivadanti; gāmabhojako āha: “veraṃ verena na sammati.”
Second Example Sentence: Rājā paccatthike jitvāpi veraṃ verena na sammatīti dūtaṃ pesesi.
Third Example Sentence: Bhikkhu kalahaṃ sutvā ubho pakkhe ovadi: “veraṃ verena na sammati, mettaṃ bhāvetha.”

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptic nominal clause; khantī as feminine nominative subject, paramaṃ tapo as superlative predicate phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Patient endurance is a higher discipline than harsh display or anger.
Usage Contexts: monastic training; family life; workplace pressure; hardship; literary advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Canonical and commentarially resonant expression praising patience as supreme ascetic discipline.
First Example Sentence: Garahito pi paṇḍito na kujjhi, khantī paramaṃ tapoti jānanto.
Second Example Sentence: Ācariyā sissānaṃ kathesi: “khantī paramaṃ tapo; kopaṃ mā vaḍḍhetha.”
Third Example Sentence: Dukkhena phuṭṭhā upāsikā khantī paramaṃ tapoti anussari.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Exclamatory nominal clause; aniccā as plural adjective, vata as exclamatory particle, saṅkhārā as plural subject.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: All conditioned situations are unstable and should not be clung to.
Usage Contexts: bereavement; philosophy; meditation; literature; crisis reflection
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Ancient Pali formula associated with reflections on impermanence in canonical and ritual contexts.
First Example Sentence: Vihāro jiṇṇo ahosi; thero āha: “aniccā vata saṅkhārā.”
Second Example Sentence: Pitaram kālaṅkataṃ sutvā putto aniccā vata saṅkhārāti roditvāpi satiṃ labhi.
Third Example Sentence: Rukkho mahāvātena bhinno; kassako aniccā vata saṅkhārāti cintesi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative optative clause; atītaṃ as accusative object, na anvāgameyya as prohibitive optative verb with sandhi.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Do not keep chasing or reliving what has already passed.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Yaṃ atītaṃ taṃ anussaritvā na socitabbaṃ.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: From the Pali contemplative tradition on non-attachment to past and future, famously echoed in bhaddekaratta teachings.
First Example Sentence: Sahāyo purāṇaṃ dosaṃ punappunaṃ kathesi; paṇḍito āha: “atītaṃ nānvāgameyya.”
Second Example Sentence: Bhikkhu vipassanāya nisīditvā atītaṃ nānvāgameyyāti cittaṃ saṇṭhapesi.
Third Example Sentence: Janapade purāṇā hāni ahosi, atha mahājano atītaṃ nānvāgameyyāti navaṃ kammaṃ ārabhi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptic nominal clause; santutthī as feminine nominative subject, paramaṃ dhanaṃ as superlative predicate noun phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Contentment is a greater wealth than possession or status.
Usage Contexts: daily life; family; economics; ascetic teaching; gratitude
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Traditional Pali maxim linked with Buddhist praise of contentment and simple living.
First Example Sentence: Duggato pi puriso santutthī paramaṃ dhananti vatvā muducitto ahosi.
Second Example Sentence: Gahapatānī dhītaraṃ ovadi: “santutthī paramaṃ dhanaṃ, mā atilobhaṃ janayi.”
Third Example Sentence: Pabbajito appakena pi cīvarena tuṭṭho santutthī paramaṃ dhananti bhāsi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptic nominal clause; ārogyaṃ as neuter nominative subject, paramaṃ lābhaṃ as superlative predicate phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Health is the most valuable gain because other advantages depend on it.
Usage Contexts: healthcare; family advice; travel; monastic routine; public welfare
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Well-known traditional Pali saying often paired with contentment and trust in verses on welfare.
First Example Sentence: Vaidyo janakāyaṃ āha: “ārogyaṃ paramaṃ lābhaṃ; bhojanaṃ mattāya bhuñjatha.”
Second Example Sentence: Therī gilānupaṭṭhākaṃ pasaṃsi: “ārogyaṃ paramaṃ lābhaṃ, tvaṃ mahākāruṇiko asi.”
Third Example Sentence: Vāṇijo dīghaṃ addhānaṃ gantukāmo ārogyaṃ paramaṃ lābhanti sarīraṃ paṭijaggi.
